name: ai-writing-markers
description: A curated, source-backed dataset of textual markers associated with AI-generated (LLM) writing. Intended as a transparency and self-editing aid, NOT as a reliable AI detector. Presence of these markers is a weak signal, not proof of authorship.
disclaimer: AI-detection is probabilistic and error-prone. Independent studies report false-positive rates from ~5% to over 60% on genuine human writing, with non-native English and formal academic prose hit hardest. Do not use these markers, or any tool built on them, as sole evidence of misconduct.

sources.title: AI content detection in 2026: how the detectors actually work (Kompozy)
How AI Detectors Work (2026): Perplexity, Burstiness & Classifiers (TextSight)
Perplexity and Burstiness in AI Detection (eyesift)
Are AI Detectors Accurate? False Positive Rates & Real Benchmarks 2026 (HumanizeMyAI)
How Do Professors Detect AI in 2026? (thesify)
Perplexity vs Burstiness: Why AI Detectors Flag Text (Leap AI)
What Is Perplexity and Burstiness in AI Writing? 2026 Guide (Vortenza)
300+ Overused ChatGPT Words to Avoid, 2026 List (Alston Antony)
Most Common ChatGPT Words to Avoid in 2026 (Walter Writes AI)
Liang et al., GPT detectors are biased against non-native English writers, Patterns 2023
How to Tell If Something Was Written by ChatGPT (AI Free Forever)

categories.label: Overused vocabulary
categories.description: Individual words that appear in LLM output at far higher rates than in comparable human writing. A single instance means little; clustering is the signal. Overused sets drift by model era.
categories.remediation: Prefer the plain word. Replace abstract nouns and inflated verbs with concrete, specific alternatives. A word being AI-overused does NOT mean its synonyms are; do not blind-swap.

markers.note: The canonical tell; usage in academic papers spiked ~25x post-ChatGPT. Faded sharply in 2025.
As an abstract noun ('rich tapestry of').
'a testament to'.
Figurative use ('underscores the importance of'). Grok still overuses it as of 2026.
As a verb meaning 'use'.
As a verb ('harness the power of').
Usually just means 'use'.
'fostering a sense of'.
As an abstract noun ('the evolving landscape of').
'in the realm of'.
Also 'intricacies'.
Also 'meticulously'.
Meaning 'has'.
Also 'bolstered'.
Corporate jargon; near-empty of meaning.
'unlock the potential of'.

categories.label: Formulaic transitions / signposting
categories.description: Connectors that LLMs stack between nearly every sentence or paragraph, especially at the start of a sentence.
categories.remediation: Delete most of them. Let logical order carry the flow, or use a concrete connective phrase tied to the actual argument.

markers.note: Especially flagged when it opens a sentence.

categories.label: Cliche phrases and stock shells
categories.description: Fixed multi-word templates that recur across AI drafts regardless of topic.
categories.remediation: Cut the shell and state the point directly; replace vague framing with a specific claim or example.

markers.term: it is important to note that

markers.note: Copula-avoidance: 'stands as / serves as' instead of 'is'.

categories.label: Structural and rhetorical tells
categories.description: Higher-order patterns detectors and human reviewers use. These require judgement, not just string matching; the checker only estimates the mechanical ones.
categories.remediation: Break parallel structures, cut outline-like restatement, replace generic hedging with a real position, and add concrete, specific detail.

markers.note: Overuse of triples: 'adjective, adjective, adjective' or 'phrase, phrase, and phrase' to make shallow analysis look thorough.
'Not only X but Y', 'It's not X, it's Y', 'no X, no Y, just Z'.
Sweeping claims about legacy/importance/broader trends ('reflects broader', 'marking a shift', 'evolving landscape').
Unsourced generalization of opinion.
Closings that pivot to 'challenges and future prospects' as a canned wrap-up.
Bulleted/numbered list where each item starts with a bold header then a colon then description. Very characteristic of chat output.

markers.regex: \b(it depends on your specific needs|there are pros and cons|varies from person to person|both have their (own )?(merits|advantages))\b
markers.note: Relentless neutrality with no stance.

categories.label: Punctuation and formatting tells
categories.description: Typography and formatting habits that recur in LLM output. Each is a weak signal on its own and has legitimate human uses.
categories.remediation: Match the surrounding document's conventions; use em dashes sparingly and without surrounding spaces where your style guide dictates; don't bold every keyword.

markers.note: LLMs use em dashes more than nonprofessional human text of the same genre, often spaced ( — ) and in a formulaic, punched-up way.
ChatGPT and DeepSeek default to curly quotes/apostrophes; Gemini and Claude usually do not. Also common in professionally typeset human text, so weak alone.
Mechanically bolding every key term in 'key takeaways' style.
Every heading in Title Case, unlike most human sentence-case prose.
Bullet-point emoji (checkmarks, rockets) used as list markers.
Reference/markup bugs pasted straight from a chatbot. A near-certain tell.

categories.label: Statistical signals
categories.description: The two classical metrics behind perplexity/burstiness detectors. True perplexity needs a reference language model; this dataset defines proxies the checker can compute locally.
categories.remediation: Raise burstiness by aggressively varying sentence length (drop a 3-8 word sentence between long ones; allow fragments). Raise perplexity with specific, less predictable word choices, not synonym swaps.

thresholds.note: Thresholds are indicative, drawn from cited 2026 write-ups, not universal constants.

markers.note: Low standard deviation of sentence length across the passage.
Predictable next-word choices. Cannot be measured without a language model; the checker reports proxies (type-token ratio, sentence-length uniformity) and says so.
